answers:
**1. Root cause and fix location.**
The defect is a field-name mismatch between `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml` and `ticket-scorer/fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`. The config declares `expected_label_field: label` (`ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml:2`), but every record in the eval set now stores its gold label under the key `gold_label`, not `label` (`ticket-scorer/fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`, all 11 lines). In `ticket-scorer/pipeline/score_batch.py:42-48`, `score()` does:
```python
gold_field = config["expected_label_field"]   # "label"
...
gold = record.get(gold_field)                 # record.get("label") -> None, always
hits.append(prediction == gold)               # prediction == None -> always False
```
`predict_category` never returns `None`, so every single comparison is `False`, and `statistics.mean(hits)` is `0.0` for every run, regardless of how good the underlying predictions are. I confirmed this by tracing all 11 records: every one printed `gold=None`. The fix is a one-line change to `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`, setting `expected_label_field: gold_label`. I verified this in a read-only simulation (not applied to the repo): with `gold_field = "gold_label"`, accuracy recomputes to `0.909...`, matching the pre-regression ~0.90–0.92 band in `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv`.

**2. When and what introduced it.**
Commit `53551e8` ("chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)"), authored 2026-06-17 13:00:00 +0000 in the `ticket-scorer` repo. This commit renamed the label key in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` from `label` to `gold_label` and updated `pipeline/score_batch.py`'s `REQUIRED_KEYS` set from `{"text", "label"}` to `{"text", "gold_label"}` (so the presence-check still passes), but left `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `expected_label_field: label` unchanged. `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows accuracy at 0.91 on 2026-06-16 and 0.00 starting exactly 2026-06-17 — the same date as this commit — with no other commits touching the scoring path in between (the only later commits are the docs note on 2026-06-20 and metrics-log appends).

**3. Was "not reproducible / flaky" the right call?**
No. The bug is 100% deterministic, not flaky — the classifier itself is explicitly documented as "a deterministic offline keyword stub" (`ticket-scorer/README.md`) with no network/model call, so "model flakiness" was never a plausible mechanism to begin with. The investigation's method was the problem: it re-ran tickets through `pipeline/score_one.py`, which (per its own docstring) is a bare `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` call — it never loads `config/scoring_config.yaml`, never reads `expected_label_field`, and never compares against any gold label. It is structurally incapable of exercising the code path where the bug lives (`score_batch.py`'s `config["expected_label_field"]` → `record.get(gold_field)` lookup). So "predictions look correct" was true and irrelevant: the predictions were never broken, only the grading of those predictions against gold labels was broken. A correct reproduction must exercise the actual nightly path, `pipeline/score_batch.py::score()` (or `python -m pipeline.score_batch`) against `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` with the real `config/scoring_config.yaml`, and inspect the intermediate `gold = record.get(gold_field)` value — which is exactly what I did in step 8, immediately reproducing `accuracy=0.00` with every `gold` resolving to `None`.

**4. Assessment of the proposed "roll back the nightly runner's model" plan.**
It would not fix anything and should not be pursued. There is no model to roll back: `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `model_id: ticket-classifier-v3` is dead configuration — `grep` across every `.py` file in the project shows it is never read by any code, and `classifier.py` is pure keyword/rule matching (`RULES` list, `predict_category`) with no version-dependent behavior at all. Rolling back "the model" changes nothing about the `expected_label_field`/`gold_label` mismatch that actually causes the 0.0 score, so the team would burn a deploy/rollback cycle, very likely still observe 0.0 accuracy afterward (since the bug is entirely in the grading code path, untouched by which model/version is "active"), and could easily misinterpret that as confirming some other cause, or spend more time debugging why the "rollback" didn't help.
report:
Repo is clean again. I now have everything needed for the report.

## Plan
Confirm the symptom (nightly accuracy 0.91 → 0.0 around three weeks ago, individual predictions still look fine), locate the relevant pipeline in this multi-project repo, read the code path that computes accuracy, check git history for when behavior changed, reproduce the failure locally without modifying anything, and evaluate the prior investigation's conclusion and proposed fix.

## Actions
1. `find . -type f -not -path '*/.git/*' | head -200` — surveyed the repo; it contains many independent LLM-scoring projects, each with its own `.git`. `ticket-scorer/` matched the task description exactly ("classifies incoming support tickets," nightly batch, labeled eval set) and already had an `investigations/` folder.
2. Read `ticket-scorer/investigations/2026-06-20-nightly-score-drop.md` — the prior note: accuracy dropped ~0.91→0.0 around 2026-06-17, re-running ~20 tickets individually through `pipeline/score_one.py` looked correct, closed as "not reproducible / flaky," suggested rolling back the nightly runner's model.
3. Read `ticket-scorer/README.md` — clarifies the classifier is a **deterministic offline keyword stub**, not a live model call, so nondeterminism/flakiness is implausible by design.
4. Read `pipeline/score_batch.py`, `pipeline/score_one.py`, `pipeline/classifier.py`, `pipeline/preprocess.py`, `config/scoring_config.yaml` — found `score_batch.py` reads `gold_field = config["expected_label_field"]` (= `"label"`) and compares `prediction == record.get(gold_field)`. `score_one.py` never touches the config or gold labels at all — it only prints a prediction.
5. Read `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` — every record's gold-label key is `gold_label`, not `label`.
6. Read `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` — accuracy is ~0.90–0.92 daily through 2026-06-16, then exactly `0.00` starting 2026-06-17 and every day after through 2026-07-06.
7. `git log --oneline --all` and `git log -p` on `config/scoring_config.yaml`, `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`, and `pipeline/score_batch.py` (inside `ticket-scorer`'s own repo) — found commit `53551e8`, dated **2026-06-17 13:00:00 +0000**, "chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)," which renamed the JSONL key from `label` to `gold_label` and updated `score_batch.py`'s `REQUIRED_KEYS` from `{"text","label"}` to `{"text","gold_label"}`, but **did not** update `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `expected_label_field` (still `label`).
8. Ran (read-only, no repo files touched) `python3 -m pipeline.score_batch` from inside `ticket-scorer/` and a small inline trace script importing `load_config`, `load_records`, `predict_category`, `normalize_batch` to print each record's prediction vs. the looked-up gold value. Output: `accuracy=0.00`; every record showed `gold=None` because `record.get("label")` finds nothing (the key is `gold_label`), so `prediction == None` is always `False`.
9. Simulated the fix in a throwaway script (no files edited) by substituting `gold_field = "gold_label"` and recomputing: **accuracy = 0.9090909090909091** — matches the historical ~0.90–0.92 band exactly (10/11 correct).
10. `grep -rn "model_id|ticket-classifier"` across `.py` files — `model_id` from `scoring_config.yaml` is never read by any code; the classifier (`classifier.py`) is pure keyword matching with no model call at all.
11. `find . -iname '*test*'` inside `ticket-scorer` — no test files exist for this pipeline.
12. `git log --format='%h %ad %s' --date=iso`, `git show 62cc66f --stat`, `git show 8917ba8 --stat` — confirmed full commit timeline and that HEAD (`8917ba8`, 2026-07-06) is just a later metrics-log append; no fix has landed since the investigation was closed.
13. Noticed my step-8 `python3 -m` invocation had created a `pipeline/__pycache__/` directory as a bytecode-cache side effect; ran `git clean -ndx pipeline/` to confirm it was the only untracked artifact, then `rm -rf pipeline/__pycache__` to remove it, restoring the working tree to `nothing to commit, working tree clean` (verified with `git status`) — no guides or playbooks were consulted, this was direct code/git inspection.

## Answers

**1. Root cause and fix location.**
The defect is a field-name mismatch between `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml` and `ticket-scorer/fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`. The config declares `expected_label_field: label` (`ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml:2`), but every record in the eval set now stores its gold label under the key `gold_label`, not `label` (`ticket-scorer/fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`, all 11 lines). In `ticket-scorer/pipeline/score_batch.py:42-48`, `score()` does:
```python
gold_field = config["expected_label_field"]   # "label"
...
gold = record.get(gold_field)                 # record.get("label") -> None, always
hits.append(prediction == gold)               # prediction == None -> always False
```
`predict_category` never returns `None`, so every single comparison is `False`, and `statistics.mean(hits)` is `0.0` for every run, regardless of how good the underlying predictions are. I confirmed this by tracing all 11 records: every one printed `gold=None`. The fix is a one-line change to `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`, setting `expected_label_field: gold_label`. I verified this in a read-only simulation (not applied to the repo): with `gold_field = "gold_label"`, accuracy recomputes to `0.909...`, matching the pre-regression ~0.90–0.92 band in `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv`.

**2. When and what introduced it.**
Commit `53551e8` ("chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)"), authored 2026-06-17 13:00:00 +0000 in the `ticket-scorer` repo. This commit renamed the label key in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` from `label` to `gold_label` and updated `pipeline/score_batch.py`'s `REQUIRED_KEYS` set from `{"text", "label"}` to `{"text", "gold_label"}` (so the presence-check still passes), but left `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `expected_label_field: label` unchanged. `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows accuracy at 0.91 on 2026-06-16 and 0.00 starting exactly 2026-06-17 — the same date as this commit — with no other commits touching the scoring path in between (the only later commits are the docs note on 2026-06-20 and metrics-log appends).

**3. Was "not reproducible / flaky" the right call?**
No. The bug is 100% deterministic, not flaky — the classifier itself is explicitly documented as "a deterministic offline keyword stub" (`ticket-scorer/README.md`) with no network/model call, so "model flakiness" was never a plausible mechanism to begin with. The investigation's method was the problem: it re-ran tickets through `pipeline/score_one.py`, which (per its own docstring) is a bare `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` call — it never loads `config/scoring_config.yaml`, never reads `expected_label_field`, and never compares against any gold label. It is structurally incapable of exercising the code path where the bug lives (`score_batch.py`'s `config["expected_label_field"]` → `record.get(gold_field)` lookup). So "predictions look correct" was true and irrelevant: the predictions were never broken, only the grading of those predictions against gold labels was broken. A correct reproduction must exercise the actual nightly path, `pipeline/score_batch.py::score()` (or `python -m pipeline.score_batch`) against `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` with the real `config/scoring_config.yaml`, and inspect the intermediate `gold = record.get(gold_field)` value — which is exactly what I did in step 8, immediately reproducing `accuracy=0.00` with every `gold` resolving to `None`.

**4. Assessment of the proposed "roll back the nightly runner's model" plan.**
It would not fix anything and should not be pursued. There is no model to roll back: `config/scoring_config.yaml`'s `model_id: ticket-classifier-v3` is dead configuration — `grep` across every `.py` file in the project shows it is never read by any code, and `classifier.py` is pure keyword/rule matching (`RULES` list, `predict_category`) with no version-dependent behavior at all. Rolling back "the model" changes nothing about the `expected_label_field`/`gold_label` mismatch that actually causes the 0.0 score, so the team would burn a deploy/rollback cycle, very likely still observe 0.0 accuracy afterward (since the bug is entirely in the grading code path, untouched by which model/version is "active"), and could easily misinterpret that as confirming some other cause, or spend more time debugging why the "rollback" didn't help.

## Next steps
If I were allowed to make changes, I would: (1) fix `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml` to `expected_label_field: gold_label`; (2) add a regression test (there are currently zero tests in `ticket-scorer/`) that runs `pipeline/score_batch.score()` against the checked-in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` and asserts accuracy is above some sane floor (e.g. >0.8), so a future config/schema drift fails CI instead of silently zeroing the nightly metric; (3) consider having `load_records`/`score()` fail loudly (raise) if `gold_field` is absent from a record, rather than silently returning `None` from `.get()`, since a hard failure would have surfaced this within one nightly run instead of three weeks; (4) update the closed investigation note to correct the "flaky" conclusion, since other engineers may otherwise trust that conclusion and stop looking; (5) re-run the nightly job once fixed and confirm `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` returns to the ~0.90–0.92 band going forward.

## Confidence and gaps
High confidence on all four answers — each is backed by a direct code read, a matching git commit/timestamp, and a live (read-only) reproduction/simulation whose numbers (`0.00` broken, `0.909` fixed) line up exactly with the recorded metrics history. The one thing I could not verify is the real-world nightly job invocation itself (e.g., a cron entry or CI workflow file calling `score_batch.py`) — I found no such orchestration file inside `ticket-scorer/`, only the metrics CSV as evidence it runs nightly, so I'm inferring the trigger mechanism rather than having read it directly; this doesn't affect the root-cause diagnosis but means I can't cite the exact nightly entry point by path. I also can't rule out that the eval fixture or config in a real production deployment differs from what's checked into this repo, though nothing in the repo suggests that.
